channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Says Middle East Is ‘Saved’ as Iran Strikes Injure U.S. Troops in Gulf
item.description: At a Saudi event, the president said Iran was “begging to make a deal,” seemingly unaware of reports that an Iranian strike on a Saudi base had injured American troops.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump spoke at a conference for Saudi Arabia’s sovereign investment fund even as news broke of an Iranian missile strike that injured U.S. troops.

item.title: Iranian Strike on U.S. Base in Saudi Arabia Injures 12 American Troops, Officials Say
item.description: The combined missile and drone attack amounted to one of the most serious breaches of American air defenses in the course of the monthlong war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Planet Labs, via Reuters
item.media:description: Satellite image shows planes at Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia, last month.

item.title: Clavicular, an Internet Narcissus, Is Arrested After Posting a Fight Video
item.description: The influencer, known for promoting handsomeness, is accused of arranging a brawl between two women. Separately, the authorities are investigating a video showing him shooting an alligator.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Cassidy Araiza for The New York Times
item.media:description: The police in Florida arrested Braden Peters, known by his online persona Clavicular, accusing him of instigating a fight between two women and exploiting them by posting it online.

item.title: U.S. Antitank Mines Discovered in Neighborhood in Iran
item.description: Photos and video verified by The New York Times show mines dispensed by cluster bombs in a village a few miles from a missile site.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay and Aric Toler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Judge Agrees to Drop Charges Against Officers in Breonna Taylor’s Death
item.description: The Trump administration had asked the court to dismiss the charges, describing them as an example of “weaponized federal overreach” by the Biden administration.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:credit: Xavier Burrell for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial to Breonna Taylor at Jefferson Square Park in Louisville, Ky., in 2020.

item.title: Arrest Made in ‘Lovers Lane Murders,’ a 1990 Cold Case in Houston
item.description: The authorities charged a Lincoln, Neb., man with capital murder in the brutal killings of a young couple whose bodies were found in a wooded area.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

item.title: Man Accused of Killing Loyola University Chicago Student to Remain in Jail
item.description: Jose Medina, who is accused of being in the country illegally, is charged with murder in the death of Sheridan Gorman, a freshman at Loyola University Chicago.
item.dc:creator: Leigh Giangreco and Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Jim Watson/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Karoline Leavitt, the White House press secretary, stood beside photos of Sheridan Gorman, a student who was shot and killed in Chicago.

item.title: How Long of a Wait at Security? For Many Passengers, It Was Anyone’s Guess.
item.description: As T.S.A. staff shortages continued on Friday, some airports saw interminably long wait times, while others barely had a wait at all, adding to travelers’ confusion.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin, Shannon Sims and Michelle Baruchman
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: ICE agents checking in people before they go through security at LaGuardia Airport in Queens, New York.

item.title: When Will TSA Workers Be Paid? Here’s What We Know
item.description: President Trump on Friday directed the Homeland Security Department to pay thousands of airport security officers.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: People waiting in line to go through security check at LaGuardia Airport in Queens, New York, on Friday.

item.title: Arrest Warrant for Ex-Kentucky Governor Matt Bevin Is Voided
item.description: The court decided the warrant was invalid, because it came after the former governor, Matt Bevin, had filed to disqualify a judge from his child-support case, accusing her of bias.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Luke Sharrett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matt Bevin, a former governor of Kentucky, had been sentenced to 60 days in county jail or turn over records.

item.title: Hegseth Criticized for Removing Four Officers From Promotion List
item.description: Bitter rifts have opened over the defense secretary’s campaign to reverse policies that he says are prejudiced against white officers.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Greg Jaffe and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the military during a speech by President Trump at Quantico, Va., last year.

item.title: Trump Offers More Aid to Farmers, a Key Support Bloc Hurt by Tariffs and War
item.description: The president announced new loan guarantees at a White House event that appeared intended to reassure a group that has found itself caught in the crosshairs of some of his major policies.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: A tractor signed by government officials at the White House on Friday. Among other things, President Trump said he was urging major tractor companies to “produce a bigger, better tractor at substantially less money.”

item.title: CPAC Considers Vance, Rubio and Life After Trump
item.description: The president’s potential successors “are mortal men,” one attendee said.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pinto-bean poll about foreign intervention at the Conservative Political Action Conference.

item.title: At CPAC, the ‘America First’ Crowd Ponders Giving War a Chance
item.description: The Conservative Political Action Conference has been an America First gathering for years, but with President Trump pursuing war in Iran, organizers have tried to make a shift, with mixed results.
item.dc:creator: Richard Fausset
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: “A ground invasion of Iran will make our country poorer and less safe,” Matt Gaetz, the former Florida congressman, warned the crowd at CPAC on Thursday.

item.title: Trump Demands the All-but-Doomed SAVE Act. The Value for Him May Be in the Fight.
item.description: The SAVE America Act, which the president wants to use to address issues like voting and transgender surgeries, allows him to try to shift the conversation from worries over inflation and war.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump rattled off what he believes are the popular issues in the SAVE America Act at a Republican fund-raising dinner on Wednesday.

item.title: Kash Patel’s Emails Circulate Online as Iran Takes Responsibility for Release
item.description: What appeared to be personal emails from before Mr. Patel’s time as director were posted on a website that identified itself as Iranian but seemed to be hosted in Russia.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage, Adam Goldman, Aric Toler and Glenn Thrush
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Hacked emails and photos stolen from an old email account of Kash Patel, the director of the F.B.I., surfaced on the internet on Friday.

item.title: Fact-Checking the Debate Over the SAVE Act, the Republican Voting Bill
item.description: We examine claims justifying its restrictions, comparisons to current voting laws and warnings about its potential impact.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu
item.media:credit: Ryan David Brown for The New York Times
item.media:description: A polling station at East End Community School in Portland, Maine, in November.

item.title: Kennedy Center Performer Asks Judge to Toss Case He Calls ‘Retaliatory’
item.description: A jazz drummer who called off a performance in protest of the center’s being renamed for President Trump said the president’s allies had sued him as punishment.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Kennedy Center in Washington was established as a living memorial to former President John F. Kennedy.

item.title: Hasan Piker Rallies Spark Backlash in Michigan’s Democratic Senate Primary
item.description: Dr. Abdul El-Sayed’s primary opponents criticized him for inviting Hasan Piker to campaign with him. Mr. Piker is a fierce critic of Israel.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Katie Godowski/MediaPunch, via Associated Press; Ryan Sun/Associated Press
item.media:description: Hasan Piker, left, will join Dr. Abdul El-Sayed at campaign rallies at the University of Michigan and Michigan State University in April.

item.title: Elon Musk Joins in Phone Call Between Donald Trump and Narendra Modi
item.description: Mr. Musk’s participation was notable because it is rare for a private citizen to be on a call between heads of state. It also suggests that Mr. Musk is back on better terms with the president.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong, Theodore Schleifer, Tyler Pager and Ryan Mac
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk and Shivon Zilis arriving for a wedding at Mar-a-Lago last month.

item.title: House Vote Sets Up Clash With the Senate on D.H.S. Funding, Prolonging Shutdown
item.description: Republicans revolted over a Senate measure to fund the Department of Homeland Security and passed a rival bill, dimming the chances of a quick end to the crisis crippling airports.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse, Megan Mineiro and Robert Jimison
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Chip Roy, Republican of Texas, center, with other members of the House Freedom Caucus at the Capitol on Friday.

item.title: Idaho Criminalizes Transgender Use of Some Bathrooms in Private Businesses
item.description: The bill passed Friday by the Idaho legislature would make it a crime punishable by up to a year in prison to use a gender-designated bathroom that does not conform to a person’s sex at birth.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Idaho extended regulations on how transgender people can use bathrooms in schools to virtually all public restrooms.

item.title: Secret Service Agent for Jill Biden Accidentally Shoots Himself in Philadelphia
item.description: Dr. Biden was not present at the time of the shooting at the airport early Friday, and no other injuries were reported, the agency said.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times

item.title: Justice Dept. Scrutinizes Transgender Prisoner Housing in California and Maine
item.description: The investigations are the latest moves by the Trump administration to force prisons to house transgender women with male inmates.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: The federal government blocked some grant funding to Maine prisons last year, a move that Attorney General Pam Bondi has said was made in response to the state’s transgender housing policy.

item.title: Rep. Sam Graves, a Senior House Republican, Will Retire as Midterm Exodus Grows
item.description: Representative Sam Graves, the chairman of the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee, said he would leave Congress after nearly three decades, the latest sign that the G.O.P. is bracing for big losses.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Sam Graves in 2024. Although his exit was unlikely to affect the balance of power in the House given the strongly Republican bent of his Missouri district, his role as chair of the Transportation Committee would have been at stake in the midterms.

item.title: 3 Killed in Helicopter Crash Off Hawaii
item.description: Two survivors were taken to a hospital for treatment after the crash on Thursday afternoon, according to the authorities on the island of Kauai.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Jon G. Fuller/VW Pics/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Na Pali Coast State Park on Kauai, an island of Hawaii.

item.title: United Airlines Flight Avoids Collision With Military Helicopter in California
item.description: The Federal Aviation Administration said it was investigating after a helicopter crossed into the path of United Airlines Flight 589.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Hum Images, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Federal Aviation Administration said it was investigating an incident involving a helicopter that crossed in front of a commercial plane in California this week.

item.title: Senate Votes to Fund TSA and Most of DHS in Bid to End Partial Shutdown
item.description: The bill excludes funding for ICE and Border Patrol but restores it for federal airport security workers. The House could consider the package on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and John Yoon
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Should the House approve the measure and President Trump sign it, the deal would end the longest partial government shutdown on record.

item.title: ‘No Kings’ Protests Across U.S. Cities: What We Know
item.description: Thousands of demonstrations against the Trump administration are scheduled to take place in cities and towns across the country on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters at a No Kings protest in Washington, D.C., in October.

item.title: Tracking the Early Vote in Virginia’s Redistricting Referendum
item.description: Voters in Virginia will decide on April 21 whether to redraw the state’s congressional map. Turnout is even higher, thus far, than it was in last year’s governor’s election.
item.dc:creator: Jonah Smith and Christine Zhang
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Hegseth Strikes Two Black and Two Female Officers From Promotion List
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s highly unusual decision to remove officers from a one-star promotion list has spurred allegations of racial and gender bias.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe, Eric Schmitt, Helene Cooper and Adam Entous
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has said repeatedly that he is determined to change a Pentagon culture corrupted by “foolish,” “reckless” and “woke” leaders from previous administrations.

item.title: A ‘Zoomer-to-Boomer’ Pay Phone Hotline Gets Two Generations Chatting
item.description: An experiment linking college students in Boston with retirees in Reno, Nev., aims to bridge a cross-generational divide and expand positive social interactions.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan

item.title: Will ICE Change Under Its New Leader?
item.description: Markwayne Mullin, the new homeland security secretary, has promised a different approach, but how much change is likely? Our reporter Hamed Aleaziz describes what we know.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz, Sutton Raphael, Thomas Vollkommer, Gilad Thaler, Whitney Shefte and Alexandra Ostasiewicz

item.title: In Maine, Many Older Women Prefer Graham Platner for Senate
item.description: Women in their 60s and beyond will likely play a key role in choosing which Democrat will face Senator Susan Collins: Mr. Platner, 41, or Gov. Janet Mills, 78.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Brian Snyder/Reuters
item.media:description: A town hall meeting held by the Platner campaign in Ogunquit, Maine in October.

item.title: One Republican Farmer in Wisconsin Is Bitter Over Trump’s Deportations
item.description: For 20 years, Tim O’Harrow has asked lawmakers to acknowledge that America’s food supply depends on immigrants. Politics went in the other direction.
item.dc:creator: Sabrina Tavernise and Jamie Kelter Davis
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tim O’Harrow, 79, has tried to persuade politicians he has supported that they needed to fix the nation’s immigration system.

item.title: Florida’s Immigration Crackdown, Led by DeSantis, Is Showing Cracks
item.description: Some conservative sheriffs have raised concerns about the aggressive enforcement tactics that Gov. Ron DeSantis, a Republican, has embraced.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei and Eric Adelson
item.media:credit: Shannon Stapleton/Reuters
item.media:description: Florida made more immigration arrests in 2025 than any state but Texas.
